complete-search.description: Enumerate every possible solution systematically. Used when n is small enough to afford exponential or factorial complexity.

example.title: Generate all subsets of {1, 2, 3}
example.explanation: Use a bitmask from 0 to 2^n - 1. Each bit position determines whether the corresponding element is included. For n=3 we have 2³=8 subsets.

divide-conquer.description: Recursively split a problem into independent sub-problems, solve each, and combine results. Canonical example: merge sort.

example.title: Merge Sort — sort [38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10]
example.explanation: Split into halves recursively until single elements. Merge sorted halves pairwise. Recurrence: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) → O(n log n).

greedy.description: Make the locally optimal choice at each step. Correct only when the greedy choice property and optimal substructure hold.

example.title: Activity Selection — pick maximum non-overlapping activities
example.explanation: Sort by end time. Greedily select each activity whose start ≥ last selected end time. Exchange argument proves this is optimal.

dynamic-programming-intro.description: Cache overlapping sub-problems. Two approaches: top-down memoization and bottom-up tabulation.

example.title: Fibonacci with memoization vs naive recursion
example.explanation: Naive recursion recomputes fib(3) multiple times. Memoization stores each result once. dp[0]=0, dp[1]=1, dp[i]=dp[i-1]+dp[i-2].

brute-force.description: Try the most direct solution without optimisation. Always correct; feasible only for small n.

example.title: Maximum Subarray Sum — O(n²) brute force
example.explanation: Fix each start index i. Extend end index j rightward, accumulating running sum. Track the global maximum. O(n²) with running sum, O(n³) recomputing from scratch.

counting-sort.description: Non-comparison sort for bounded integers. Count occurrences, prefix-sum, place elements. O(n+k) time.

example.title: Sort [4, 2, 2, 8, 3, 3, 1] with values in range [1,8]
example.explanation: Build count[1..8]. Apply prefix sums so count[v] = number of elements ≤ v. Iterate input backward for stability, placing each element at its prefix-sum position.

radix-sort.description: Sort integers digit by digit using a stable sort (counting sort) at each digit position. LSD variant processes least significant digit first.

example.title: LSD Radix Sort on [170, 45, 75, 90, 802, 24, 2, 66]

ternary-search.description: Find the extremum of a unimodal function by discarding one-third of the interval each iteration.

example.title: Minimise f(x) = (x-3)² + 2 on [0, 10]
example.explanation: Each iteration evaluates f(m1) and f(m2) at two interior points. Discard the third where f is larger. Repeat until hi-lo < epsilon. O(log n) iterations.

bit-manipulation.description: Direct operations on binary representations: AND, OR, XOR, shifts. Used for subset enumeration, fast flags, and optimised DP.

example.title: Common bitmask tricks
example.output: First set bit = 2 | Last set bit = 4 | Popcount = 2
example.explanation: n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit. n & (-n) isolates the lowest set bit. __builtin_popcount(n) counts set bits. These O(1) ops underpin fast subset enumeration.
example.code:
int n = 18;          // 10010 in binary
int lsb = n & (-n);  // 00010 → isolate lowest set bit
n & (n-1);           // 10000 → clear lowest set bit
__builtin_popcount(n); // 2   → count of set bits
for(int s=mask;s;s=(s-1)&mask) { /* enumerate submasks */ }

trie.description: A prefix tree where each node represents one character. Supports O(L) insert, search, and prefix queries.

example.title: Insert 'cat', 'car', 'card' into a trie
example.output: Trie stores shared prefix 'ca' once; branches at 't' and 'r'; 'r' has child 'd'
example.explanation: Root → c → a → t (end of 'cat'). From 'a' also → r (end of 'car') → d (end of 'card'). Prefix search for 'car' traverses 3 nodes. Total nodes = 6.

trie-operations.description: Insert, search, delete, and prefix-count operations on a trie. Each costs O(L) where L is the string length.

example.title: Count words with prefix 'ca' in trie
example.explanation: Augment each node with a count field incremented on insert. Traverse the prefix path 'c'→'a'. The count at node 'a' gives the number of strings sharing this prefix.

string-hashing.description: Map a string to an integer using a polynomial rolling hash. Enables O(1) substring equality checks after O(n) preprocessing.

example.title: Polynomial rolling hash of 'hello'
example.explanation: Precompute prefix hashes and powers. Hash of s[l..r] = (hash[r+1] - hash[l]*pow[r-l+1]) mod M. Comparison is O(1). Use double hashing to reduce collision probability.

z-algorithm.description: Computes Z-array where Z[i] = length of the longest substring starting at i that matches a prefix of the string. Enables O(n) pattern matching.

example.title: Find pattern 'ab' in text 'ababcabab'
example.explanation: Concatenate s = pattern + '$' + text. Compute Z-array. Any position i where Z[i] == len(pattern) is a match. '$' separator prevents Z-values crossing the boundary.

prime-numbers.description: Sieve of Eratosthenes finds all primes up to n in O(n log log n). Segmented sieve handles large ranges.

example.title: Sieve of Eratosthenes up to n=30
example.explanation: Start with all numbers marked prime. For each prime p, mark p², p²+p, p²+2p, ... as composite. Only iterate p up to sqrt(n). Mark[i] stays true only for primes.

factors.description: Find all factors of n in O(√n) by iterating up to √n and pairing each divisor with n/divisor.

example.title: All factors of n = 36
example.explanation: Iterate i from 1 to √36=6. If i divides 36, add both i and 36/i. At i=6, 36/6=6 so add only once. Result sorted: {1,2,3,4,6,9,12,18,36}.

modular-arithmetic.description: Compute (a^b) mod m in O(log b) using fast exponentiation. Modular inverse via Fermat's little theorem when m is prime.

example.title: Compute 2^10 mod 1000000007
example.explanation: Binary exponentiation: 2^10 = (2^5)^2. 2^5 = (2^2)^2 × 2 = 32. 32^2 = 1024. Each step squares and optionally multiplies, halving the exponent. O(log b) multiplications.

ford-fulkerson.description: Find maximum flow in a network by repeatedly augmenting along paths from source to sink. BFS augmentation (Edmonds-Karp) gives O(VE²).

example.title: Maximum flow in a simple 4-node network
example.explanation: Augment s→A→t (flow=8). Augment s→B→t (flow=10). Total=18. Residual graph allows backward edges to enable flow redistribution in future augmentations.

flow-networks.description: A directed graph where each edge has a capacity. Model real-world constraints like bandwidth, pipes, or bipartite matching as flow networks.

example.title: Bipartite matching as max flow
example.input: Left: {A,B,C}, Right: {X,Y,Z}, Edges: A-X, A-Y, B-Y, C-Z
example.output: Maximum matching = 3 pairs: A-X, B-Y, C-Z
example.explanation: Add super-source s→{A,B,C} (cap 1 each) and super-sink {X,Y,Z}→t (cap 1 each). Run max-flow. Each unit of flow corresponds to one matched pair.

path-covers.description: Minimum path cover of a DAG: fewest paths to cover all vertices. Equals n minus maximum bipartite matching.

example.title: Minimum path cover of a 5-node DAG
example.explanation: Split each node v into v_out and v_in. Add edge v_out→w_in for each DAG edge v→w. Max bipartite matching M gives min path cover = n − |M|.

euler-paths.description: An Eulerian path visits every edge exactly once. Exists in undirected graphs when exactly 0 or 2 vertices have odd degree.

example.title: Find Eulerian circuit in the Königsberg bridge graph variant
example.explanation: All vertices have even degree (deg: 1=2,2=3 — wait, check). Hierholzer's: start at any node, follow edges until stuck, then splice in sub-circuits. O(V+E).

coin-change.description: Find minimum coins to make a target amount. Classic DP: dp[x] = min coins for amount x.

example.title: Minimum coins for target = 11 with {1, 5, 6, 9}
example.explanation: dp[0]=0. For x=1..11: dp[x] = min over all coins c of (dp[x-c]+1). dp[5]=1, dp[6]=1, dp[11]=min(dp[10]+1, dp[6]+1, dp[5]+1)=min(3,2,2)=2.

lis.description: Longest Increasing Subsequence. O(n²) DP or O(n log n) with patience sorting + binary search.

example.title: LIS of [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6]
example.explanation: O(n log n): maintain 'tails' array where tails[i] = smallest tail of any IS of length i+1. Binary search to update. tails = [1], [1,4], [1,4,5], [1,4,5,9], final length = 4.

grid-paths.description: Count or find optimal paths in a grid from top-left to bottom-right, moving only right or down.

example.title: Count paths in a 3×3 grid
example.input: 3×3 grid, move only right or down
example.explanation: dp[i][j] = number of ways to reach (i,j) = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]. Base: first row and column = 1. dp[2][2] = dp[1][2]+dp[2][1] = 3+3 = 6.

edit-distance.description: Minimum insert, delete, or replace operations to transform string s into string t. O(nm) DP.

example.title: Edit distance between 'kitten' and 'sitting'
example.explanation: kitten→sitten (replace k→s), sitten→sittin (replace e→i), sittin→sitting (insert g). dp[i][j]=edit distance for s[0..i] to t[0..j]. Transitions: match (0 cost), insert, delete, replace.

counting-tilings.description: Count ways to tile an n×m board with 1×2 dominoes using bitmask DP over columns.

example.title: Tile a 2×4 board with 1×2 dominoes
example.explanation: Profile DP: process column by column. State = bitmask of cells already filled. Transition tries all valid domino placements for current column given previous column's state.

coin-problem.description: Greedy coin selection for canonical currency systems. Sort denominations descending; take as many of the largest as possible.

example.title: Make change for 41 cents with {25, 10, 5, 1}
example.explanation: Take one 25 (41-25=16). Take one 10 (16-10=6). Take one 5 (6-5=1). Take one 1. Total = 4 coins. Greedy is optimal for this canonical system due to the exchange argument.

task-scheduling.description: Schedule jobs with deadlines and profits to maximise total profit. Greedy: sort by profit, assign each to latest available slot.

example.title: Schedule jobs: (profit, deadline) = (100,2),(19,1),(27,2),(25,1),(15,3)
example.explanation: Sort by profit descending. Try to place each job in the latest available slot ≤ its deadline. Use a disjoint-set to efficiently find the latest free slot.

minimize-sums.description: Pair elements of two arrays to minimize the sum of products. Proof: sort one ascending, the other descending, pair by index.

example.title: Minimise sum of a[i]*b[i]: a=[1,2,3], b=[4,5,6]
example.explanation: Sort a ascending and b descending, then sum a[i]*b[i]. Rearrangement inequality: this pairing minimises the dot product. Any swap increases the total.

points.description: Represent points as (x,y) pairs. Key operations: distance, cross product (turn direction), dot product (angle).

example.title: Cross product to determine orientation of 3 points
example.explanation: Cross product of PQ×PR = (Q-P) × (R-P) = (4,4)×(4,0) = 4*0-4*4 = -16. Negative = clockwise. Zero = collinear. Positive = counter-clockwise.

lines.description: Lines defined by two points or ax+by=c form. Key queries: intersection, distance from point to line, on-segment test.

example.title: Find intersection of two lines
example.input: Line 1: through (0,0) and (2,2). Line 2: through (0,2) and (2,0).
example.explanation: Line 1: y = x (i.e. x-y=0). Line 2: y = -x+2 (i.e. x+y=2). Solve: x-y=0 and x+y=2 → 2x=2 → x=1, y=1.

convex-polygon.description: Convex hull: the smallest convex polygon containing all given points. Graham scan or Andrew's monotone chain runs in O(n log n).

example.title: Convex hull of 6 points
example.explanation: Andrew's monotone chain: sort by x then y. Build lower hull (left turns only), then upper hull. Remove points that cause right turns (clockwise) as you add each new point.

concave-polygon.description: A polygon that is not convex (has at least one interior angle > 180°). Point-in-polygon test uses ray casting.

example.title: Ray casting: is point (3,3) inside polygon?
example.explanation: Cast a horizontal ray from the query point to the right. Count how many polygon edges it crosses. Odd count = inside. Even count = outside. Handle edge cases (ray through vertex).

distance-formula.description: Euclidean distance √((x₂-x₁)²+(y₂-y₁)²). Prefer squared distance to avoid floating-point issues when only comparison is needed.

example.title: Distance between (1,2) and (4,6)
example.explanation: dx=4-1=3, dy=6-2=4. distance=√(3²+4²)=√(9+16)=√25=5. This is a 3-4-5 Pythagorean triple. Squared distance = 25 (integer, avoids sqrt).

all-subsets.description: Generate all 2ⁿ subsets of a set using bitmasks or recursive backtracking.

example.title: All subsets of {A, B, C}
example.explanation: Bitmask: iterate mask from 0 to 2³-1. Each bit determines inclusion. Recursive: at each index, choose include or exclude, recurse. Both give identical results.

all-permutations.description: Generate all n! orderings of n elements using backtracking or next_permutation (C++ STL).

example.title: All permutations of {1, 2, 3}
example.explanation: At each position, try every unused element. Recursively fill remaining positions. Backtrack by restoring the element. Uses a 'used' boolean array to track availability.
